item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This actually made my day. I'm so glad you enjoyed my take on Iris Dark. I don't often play in other authors yards, so to speak, but whenever I do I like to hope I'm doing their characters, settings, or concepts justice. Your approval is deeply appreciated, Randomini :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so glad you enjoyed it! Thanks for the kind words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As the original author of Iris Dark, I enormously approve of this characterisation and manner of conveyance. And that it's left to reader interpretation how much of Iris' patter is a facade.

To the extent I can co-canonise such things, I declare this Very Canon, and exceptionally well-written to boot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have much to say aside from "good job." Rich characterization, deliciously brutal subject matter, chilling dialogue. The tale is what made it for me. +1

item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: If the post is a few weeks up and the pic isn't down, it probably is fine.

item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: It doesn't seem to be, at least in general. I'm not sure if the wiki has special rules for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey SeRa Destin, thanks for the read! I'm glad you enjoyed it :)

You can find the ACS bar here, with instructions on how to use it! http://www.scp-wiki.net/anomaly-classification-system-guide

This, and many other free-to-use resources for improving the visual style of your article can be found here. Enjoy! http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-style-resource

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"This sauce is f#@'n amazing you said it was promoting a movie?"

Literally the feeling of this sweet baby here. Amazing SCP with a genius story mate, very well done!!! Especially how the personality of Iris revealed more of herself as negotiations went on. Pure artistic genius. I love it! <3

One question tho: I am new to the SCP as a member, have been lurking for six years now. I wonder how you make these delicate classification symbols. It is something that you create yourself or is there some free-to-use image kit somehwere around? Thanks for answering and keep up the truly magnificant work!

item.title: Plot Twist
item.content:encoded: The Iris Dark shown here also plays rugby as a guilty pleasure.

item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Quick check - is 4.0 valid? If not, I can replace this image. It's not vital.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Tapioca pearls image is by Nandaro, under CC BY-SA 3.0.

Cassova root image is by CIAT, under CC BY-SA 2.0.

Kerala leaves image is by Slashme, under CC BY-SA 4.0.

item.title: Re: SCP-5579
item.content:encoded: The leaves, I can trace to https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kerala_Cassava_plant_2.jpg (correctly licensed). The others, though, I can't find with any of my image search tools. Be more specific about where you got them?

item.title: Re: Haven't finished yet but caught this
item.content:encoded:
And again I say DAMN YOU STREAM OF CONSCIOUSNESS WRITING.

I can't believe I keep missing this stuff. Thanks to both of you, it's been corrected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks so much gee! I still have to agree, though I'm having difficulty finding a way to bolster these weaker parts - the entire idea is that the primary article is a strange, incomplete story that lacks true context, later revealed through an even stranger circumstance. Still, that's not an excuse. Each section needs to be a complete story by it's own right, and while the first half does that, it's not a satisfying completion.

I'll work on it. Thanks for the read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WOW I can't believe I didn't spot that on revision. This is what I get for writing all stream-of-consciousness and not paying proper attention to the ding-dang context. Thanks for spotting this, and thanks for the read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No kidding! I had no idea there was already a similar article on the Chinese branch. Then again, it's not super surprising. I can't be the first person who's looked at tapioca pearls and thought "alien eggs".

And I'm so good at this because there's an awesome community of writers/readers here to work with and support me. Y'know, like you!

Thanks for the read pasta :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is great. The characterisation of Iris is phenomenal, and while the article mainly survives on something that's more reminiscent of a tale it's hardly an issue when the content's this good.

I'll definitely echo the points made by ARD and aismallard, since those sections are undoubtedly the weakest in this article. However, they're not on the level where they really affected my overall enjoyment, and this is a strong upvote for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Iris Black or Iris Dark? I saw both names there, I'm assuming it's a mistake. Otherwise, I love this scip for so many reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm loving this, Iris Dark's characterization is wonderfully fucked up and I'm really into it. The concept of parasitic boba tea is also an idea that I am surprised hasn't been done before here. (Although, I do know of a Chinese SCP about parasitic boba tea, but I'm talking in english branch terms.) Wonderful stuff, how are you so good at this?

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fine! The story wouldn't be complete without the second half. That said, I do agree that the initial article lacked enough punch. It's something I'm working on. Thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I've got a fondness for writing stories like this - where a more generic concept like "alien parasites" gets a few more layers to it. Thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, without the article attached to it, I probably would've no-voted. But Iris Dark added so much to this with her arrogant tone and demeaning personality that it really made the article pop for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the turnaround from generic "oh no there's big spooky aliens and they're coming for us what a clifhangerrr" to a more detailed explanation of the situation. Great article!

item.title: Re: Haven't finished yet but caught this
item.content:encoded: I think what they mean is that it says "bitter yuca" toward the top and "bitter starch" at the bottom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right!? I'm glad I'm not the only one who thinks tapioca looks, feels, and generally has a creepy vibe. I'm glad the article landed for you. Thanks for the read r0se!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks GG Crono! Love the pun, and thanks for the read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i drink (or rather drank, my local shop is closed indefinitely now) a lot of boba. this article fucking horrifies me!

conceptually, tapioca is the perfect food for this, tapioca balls look like eggs and raw tapioca is already kind of strange and misshapen. coupling this was the incredibly well written delivery of Iris Dark… it's horrifying in both a "holy shit aliens could maybe live in us" as well as a "holy shit rich people are awful" way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love those articles that grab you from the first line of the containment procedures. And it's doubly sweet when they can follow through on them. Take my emphatic upvote.

item.title: Re: Phenomenal read
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you enjoyed it SuspiciousScript! Glad to know the transmissions and scene transition didn't miss for everyone. If you have any suggestions for how to re-write these two lines to enhance the clinical tone, I'm totally open. I'll flag them for re-write if I come back to this article and clean it up. Cheers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the review Mapleseruhp! It's very helpful knowing what issues hit, and which aspects work for multiple readers. I really will have to re-visit the alien transmissions.

item.title: Phenomenal read
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this piece. Personally, I wasn't at all bothered by the transmissions or format switch; if anything, I thought the latter was an effective scene transition. This skip manages to be novel without being a novelty, which isn't easy. Thanks for writing it!

A couple minor notes:
– hide block

SCP-5579 functions similarly to a bacterial colony, expertly mimicking the polysaccharide chains present in plant starch. This level of chemical camouflage is so advanced, in fact, that Foundation scientists have only recently identified its existence through comparative study.

Yet despite all modern advantages, SCP-5579 has still failed to form a lasting parasitic relationship with any terrestrial species.

I think the clinical tone slipped a bit here; adverbs aren't generally something I associate with scientific writing. Very minor quibble, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this scp because it takes something innocuous and blows it into something extraordinary while still remaining believable. Also, even if one in a million eggs actually hatch upon consumption but still die anyway, the thought of tapioca parasites make me cringe. The broadcast wasn't necessary, their characterization as a culture is clear through the almost-tale. The extended narrative was saved by your characterization of Iris. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers! Thanks for the read :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey aismallard! I'm currently working on ways to fix these issues. They seem fairly universal across the crits I've received. I'm always open to suggestions, but Ill try and think of a better way of portraying the avanaski, transitioning to the MC&D portion, and making it a more uniform progression. Thanks for the read and the crit!

item.title: Re: Haven't finished yet but caught this
item.content:encoded: It's project "Bitter Yuca", as the foundation is trying to breed a strain of cassava root that is poison / inhospitable to the parasite. Also, trust, you don't have to worry about it being ruined for you. If anything, this article will enhance the experience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Chicken." Iris said, regardless. "They taste like dry, white chicken.”

I cackled. +1 hands down.

item.title: Haven't finished yet but caught this
item.content:encoded:
Is it project "bitter yuca" or " bitter starch"?

Also I love steamed yuca with a lot of guasacaca and grilled meat and by all I hold dear, this article won't ruin it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As mentioned above, you do Ms. Dark a real service here in how she's portrayed. Some of the sentences in here are simply excellent.

The SCP introduction of it is well-structured, with a good first-line hook and images going into its description. However I also felt the alien transmission unnecessary: the point about their shittiness to be best implied in some other way.

The "it's getting neutralized" bit is a bit sudden, and I'm not sure about the transition to the MC&D portion.

That said none of this prevented me from enjoying it, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Totally valid. The honest truth of why I went with a MC&D page shift is because that's how I originally wrote it, and translating it to a transcript felt like it muted the voices in my head? If that makes sense.

Honestly, I'll see how this does as-is, but if the crit from authors I respect continues to be this heavy, I might take it down work on the formatting, relying on a more traditional transcript / log format.

I like the characters I've created here, and I want them to resonate through the best possible written medium. If the tale isn't it, then I need to revisit this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one is hard for me. Because it's like… it kind of rides that line between humor and brutality. But my issues lie largely in execution.

While I'm not one usually to harp much on "immersion", the sudden switch from the SCP document to the tale-thing felt extremely jarring to me. Mostly because I couldn't stop asking myself:

"Why did this have to be a tale? Why couldn't you have just continued it in the skip?"

And while there's a few possible reasons, things like wanting to utilize the prose and the different colored dialogue, the thought "but why not just put this in a transcript" heavily distracted me from the actual content of the tale. And by proxy of that, I found myself hung up on how expository I felt iris was being in the end of that tale. It's not as annoying to me as most exposition because you do a good job of injecting character and voice into it, but at the end of the day I couldn't separate it from being just an explanation, with the whole time in my head asking myself "but why did you have to explain it in prose?"

All in all… it's hard for me because I wanted to enjoy this. This had a lot of things going for it from the "useless parasite" angle to the brutality of eating a species just to feel superior to it. But the presentation of it distracted me from the content because I couldn't stop asking myself "but whyyyy?"

I think I'm going to have to downvote. Not because I think this is terrible, but because I can't help but think this could've hit harder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey FourMinorSix! I'm glad you enjoyed the article. I was worried the alien transmissions were just a complete whiff, but I'm glad to know they landed for someone. I'm still considering possible alternatives for those dialogue pieces.

Also, I really appreciate you spotting those SPaG errors - I've corrected them all now. Thanks for the crit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I adore this SCP. You've got this alien race that's dying of its own insensibility, and on the other hand there's MC&D deriving twisted, hedonistic pleasure from that race's failure. It's existentially dreadful and filled with depravity on both ends. It's really impressive that you could tease that story out of tapioca, of all things.

The base anomaly was also interesting, and it had a really snappy description that was a pleasure to read. While I know other commenters found the transmissions excessive, I actually really love them. It shows how desperate, and more importantly how petulant, the SCP has become; so much of that characterisation would be lost if the messages weren't there. You'd basically be relying on Iris' monologue to characterise the SCP at that point.

Thanks for the read! This really made my day, and it's one of my favourites in Series 6 so far :)

+ Some SPaG stuff I found
- Some SPaG stuff I found

Conprocs

Project "Bitter Yuca" is currently underway breeding dominant strains of Cassava

cassava?

Description

multiple opportunities for the parasite to find a latching-point, similar to of egg-cell fertilization

remove the "of"?

this organism quickly succumbs to the hosts digestive processes

host's (with apostrophe)?

Offset 1

2. The SCP Foundation will exchange an equivilant sum of classified objects

equivalent?

"The Avanaski," Iris began "Come from a world so lush with resources
“It’s a breed of pheasant," Iris said

missing commas after "began" and "pheasant", and I think "come" should be lowercase in the first one?

"It tastes just like Tapioca. SCP-5579…"

"tapioca"?

I’d carry on and say 'The real point of Faisan Pathétique is eating something that, for one glorious moment, understood its place in the universal pecking order.”

Didn't close one pair of quotation marks there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the read Koooper! I'd pet that tiny turtle, but alas, it's a drawing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …holy shit, this is amazing. I would give this all the upvotes if I could, but alas, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Surely I'll remember this comment, years from now, in the Grey Age of Tubby, working away in the tapioca mines with my fellow dissidents.

Will I learn anything? Probably not.

Thanks for the read Niles :) I'm glad you liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always hated tapioca

Tubby is sad now, as are his many relatives.

Regardless, a very fascinating article, with Iris easily being the best part. +1

Edited to add: so THAT'S where that list of items went.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks carolynn! I'm thinking of possible alternatives for this section that still convey the same sentiment and info for the reader. I'm always open to suggestions. Either way, thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey ARD! I'm glad I've improved my characterization since my last article, though it's a two-steps-forward-one-step-back situation, as always. I wanted the 5579 transmissions to be a quick, vague impression that these creatures are technologically advanced, arrogant, and so full of entitled disdain that they'd send a follow-up message detailing how much of a shitty species we are by the metric of baby food. Basically, the alien equivalent of an xbox live message. Sorry it didn't land, but I'm really happy you liked my take on Iris.

Thanks as always for the crit, and the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i agree wholly with what ARD said above. the characterization of Dark here is fantastic, bringing a very human (and deliciously creepy) side to MC&D's avarice. while i wouldn't say the messages from their homeworld are explicitly bad, they are certainly the weakest part of the article and don't entirely add much the offset dialogue which is the meat of the article. still, Iris's characterization brings a whole new face to MC&D, one which is absolutely incredible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I deeply appreciate this variant of Iris Dark. It's a far cry from virtually all her other appearances in the wiki and one of my favorites to boot: a deeply wealthy, deeply cultured, and deeply hedonistic monster who embodies what I only recently realized was missing from Marshall, Carter, and Dark. I find the messages sent from 5579's home world to be unnecessary at best and nearly article-ruining at worst, so it's lucky that Iris overshadows them completely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you liked it, Cerastes. I'd like to use this interpretation of Iris in other articles, or tales, in future :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“It’s a breed of pheasant," Iris said "Found only on a single farm in France. The previous owner hung himself in the barn. For whatever reason, whenever a Faisan Pathétique is butchered, it experiences an instant of existential realization. It understands it’s a bird. It understands it has lived a pointless life. It even realizes it is dying purely for the sake of some human's dinner.”

Iris's dark characterization (heh) really clinched this one for me. +1

item.title: SCP-5579
item.content:encoded:
Sippin' on my bubble tea
Oh I was catching up with my homies
Aw no when suddenly she stood next to me
I couldn't help myself with this shorty

I said I just wanna kick it, sit back and just chill with
You and me we just get along
It's low-key passion, mutual attraction
Baby you and me we just get along

Thanks to: ROUNDERHOUSE, AbsentmindedNihilist, MalyceGraves, Sevatar, Popsioak, DrMoned and Crusty_Jon.

Images used are from Wikicommons, found here,here, and here.

I've always hated tapioca. I'm very much a texture-eater, and those little baubles have always looked and felt like slimy little alien eggs to me. My partner, however, loves them, and always makes a point to noisily slurp her bubble tea around me. You can guess what inspired this article.

As always, thank you for reading.

Side-note. This Iris Black isn't intended to mirror the Iris Black in other MC&D articles. She's my interpretation of the character - someone older, someone different. Marshall, Carter, and Black are all fantastic characters, but I can never seem to write within the lines of another authors narrative. That said, I whole heatedly encourage you to explore the MC&D Canon, and the many fantastic articles and tales within.

Cheers!

► More T Rutherford Articles and Tales ◄
